text: The surgeon performs medial dissection of the colon, separating it from the Gerota's fascia. Blunt dissection is used to identify and dissect the right gonadal vein and ureter distally. The dissection proceeds cranially towards the renal hilum, where the renal vessels are circumferentially isolated. The kidney is dissected from its cranial and lateral peritoneal attachments and mobilized posteriorly to facilitate flipping and exposure of the posteriorly located tumor.

The surgeon fully mobilizes the kidney within the Gerota's fascia and flips it postero-inferiorly, exposing the tumor. The tumor borders are clearly delineated, with renal fat spared over the lesion. The periphery of the mass is scored using cautery. The renal artery and vein are clamped individually, and the mass is excised from the kidney using sharp cold laparoscopic scissors, maintaining a surgical margin of approximately five millimeters around the tumor.

The surgeon performs renorrhaphy using a two-layer sliding clip technique with Vicryl 2-0 round sutures and Hem-o-lok clips. The renal vessels are unclamped, and the sutures are further secured with Liga clips. The Gerota's fascia is closed with interrupted Vicryl 2-0 sutures and Hem-o-lok clips.

The excised renal mass is placed into a laparoscopic retrieval bag. A Jackson-Pratt drain is positioned within the right paracolic gutter, and liver retraction is released. The laparoscopic bag containing the mass is extracted through the 12mm port site.
